musicalLanguage.description: Robert Johnson recorded 'Sweet Home Chicago' in 1936, and it's since become one of the most-covered blues songs in history — a steady, driving boogie-woogie shuffle built around the migration narrative of heading north to Chicago, later popularized further by the Blues Brothers. This arrangement sits in E major at a walking 121 BPM across 135 measures, and its energy is clearly organized around its ending: the single busiest measure in the whole piece (measure 120, 29 onsets) falls near the close, and a recurring pattern (measures 39/40 repeating at 99/100, a 60-measure interval — likely five 12-bar choruses) shows the boogie bassline cycling steadily underneath.
musicalLanguage.structure: A boogie-woogie 12-bar blues built on E7-A7-B7 (I-IV-V) cycling repeatedly across the form, with the note data confirming the recurring unit directly: measures 39-40 reappear as an exact and near-identical repeat at measures 99-100, 60 measures later (five 12-bar choruses at 12 measures each). Energy climbs steadily and peaks late — measure 120 is the single busiest in the arrangement — before the texture thins dramatically in the final measures: 134 and 135 drop to 0 and 1 onsets respectively, a soft fade after the climax rather than a hard stop.
keyMoments: Measures 39-40 repeat almost exactly at measures 99-100, 60 measures later — five 12-bar boogie choruses apart, confirming the driving E7 bassline cycles through the same figure chorus after chorus.
Measure 120 is the busiest in the entire piece (29 onsets) and arrives late, in the piece's final quarter — the arrangement saves its highest energy for near the end.
The right hand's highest note, G6, also arrives at measure 119 — one measure before the density peak, meaning the registral and rhythmic climaxes land right next to each other.
Measures 134 and 135, the final two measures, drop to 0 and 1 onsets — after the measure-120 peak, the piece fades out rather than ending on a hard final E7 chord.
Measures 1-2 open at low density (2 and 4 onsets) before the boogie pattern locks in, which demonstrates how a groove can establish itself gradually rather than starting at full strength.
teachingGoals: Boogie-woogie bassline: practice the recurring left-hand E7 figure that reappears at measures 39-40 and 99-100 until the 60-measure gap between repeats doesn't matter — your hand should already know it.
12-bar blues chorus counting: with the same E7-A7-B7 unit repeating every 60 measures (five choruses), practice counting choruses rather than measures to keep your place in a long boogie form.
Late-arriving climax pacing: measure 120's peak comes in the final quarter — build the discipline to keep the groove interesting through 100-plus measures before that payoff arrives.
Fading endings: the piece dissolves rather than stops — practice a controlled decrescendo and pedal release for the final two measures instead of a cut-off.
styleTips: Keep the boogie-woogie left hand rock-steady and physical — it's the propulsive engine of the whole 135-measure arrangement and can't be allowed to drift even once the right hand gets busy.
Save real intensity for measure 120 — with the busiest measure landing that late, resist maxing out the dynamics in the first half.
Play the ending so it genuinely fades — ease off both hands together through measures 134-135 rather than playing a definitive final chord the data doesn't support.
Use the first two measures to settle the groove gently (they're noticeably sparser) before you play into full boogie energy.
